etymology.description: 入(入) (rù, "enter") + 一(一) (yī, "one") = gathering from three sides -> original form of 今(今) (jīn, "now")
etymology.interpretation: 亼(亼) (jī, "open mouth flipped upside down") originally represented the action of gathering, with 入(入) (rù, "enter") suggesting bringing things together and 一(一) (yī, "one") implying unification into a single entity. This concept aligns traditionally with 集(集) (jí, "gather"), where the sense of gathering from three sides connects to assembling or coming together. The visual comparison to an open mouth reversed is a less common interpretation, relating to a shape rather than function.
